howItWorks.intro: This calculator estimates daily seed oil consumption based on dining habits, cooking choices, and packaged food intake.
rules: Restaurant meals: most restaurants cook with soybean or canola oil — estimated 2-4 tbsp seed oil per meal
Cooking oil: home cooking with seed oils adds ~1-2 tbsp per meal; olive oil, butter, and coconut oil contain minimal linoleic acid
Packaged foods: most contain soybean, canola, or sunflower oil — ~0.5-1 tbsp seed oil per serving
Fried/snack foods: chips, fries, and fried foods are major sources — 1-3 tbsp per serving
Dressings and sauces: most commercial dressings are soybean oil-based — ~0.5-1 tbsp per serving
Linoleic acid: the primary omega-6 fatty acid in seed oils, making up 50-60% of soybean and corn oil
howItWorks.outro: The average American consumes roughly 40-80g of seed oil per day. Ancestral diets contained far less omega-6. Whether this matters for health is actively debated — consult your healthcare provider.

faq.question: What are seed oils?
faq.answer: Seed oils are vegetable oils extracted from seeds using industrial processing — including soybean oil, canola (rapeseed) oil, corn oil, sunflower oil, safflower oil, grapeseed oil, and cottonseed oil. They are high in omega-6 polyunsaturated fatty acids, particularly linoleic acid.

faq.question: Why are people concerned about seed oils?
faq.answer: Critics point to the dramatic increase in seed oil consumption since the 1900s (from ~2% to ~20% of calories) and correlation with rising chronic disease. The concern centers on high omega-6 to omega-3 ratios potentially promoting inflammation. The science is still debated.

faq.question: What is a healthy omega-6 to omega-3 ratio?
faq.answer: Ancestral diets had ratios of roughly 1:1 to 4:1 (omega-6 to omega-3). Modern Western diets are estimated at 15:1 to 20:1 or higher. Many health researchers suggest aiming for a ratio below 4:1, though optimal levels are debated.

faq.question: How much seed oil does the average American consume?
faq.answer: USDA data shows Americans consume approximately 40-80 grams of added fats and oils per day, with soybean oil being the dominant source. This translates to roughly 3-6 tablespoons of seed oil daily, mostly from restaurant food and packaged products.

faq.question: What oils are NOT seed oils?
faq.answer: Non-seed oils include olive oil, coconut oil, avocado oil, butter, ghee, tallow, lard, and palm oil. These are either fruit oils, animal fats, or tropical oils and generally have different fatty acid profiles with less linoleic acid.

faq.question: How can I reduce seed oil intake?
faq.answer: Cook at home with olive oil, butter, coconut oil, or avocado oil. Read labels on packaged foods. Ask restaurants what oil they cook with. Make your own salad dressings. Choose whole foods over processed ones. When eating out, opt for grilled over fried.
